configure-and-initialize-a-repository.label: Initialize Your First Repository
configure-and-initialize-a-repository.summary: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.

sketch0.title: Create the Repository in the Right Place

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Initialize an Ordinary Folder
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

The folder already contains a README but has no history. Initialize Git with `main` as the first branch. This exercise asks for only the initialization step.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Choose the initial branch while Git creates the repository metadata.

help.concept: `git init -b main` creates repository metadata in the current folder and names its first branch in one step.
help.hint_1: The terminal is already inside the project folder, so do not change directories.
help.hint_2: The task asks for one action: initialize this folder with the requested first branch.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

The folder already contains a README but has no history. Initialize Git with `main` as the first branch. This exercise asks for only the initialization step.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you initialize this project folder with `main` as its first branch?

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Read What Git Created
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

`trail-journal/` is now a repository. List its `.git` directory, then read `.git/HEAD` to confirm that HEAD points to `main`.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Inspect Git’s private metadata without editing it.

help.concept: The .git directory stores repository metadata, and HEAD identifies the currently checked-out branch reference.
help.hint_1: Look for the file that records the current branch reference.
help.hint_2: Confirm that the reference ends with the requested branch name.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

`trail-journal/` is now a repository. List its `.git` directory, then read `.git/HEAD` to confirm that HEAD points to `main`.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C trail-journal init -q -b main

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C trail-journal init -q -b main

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you inspect the repository metadata and confirm what `HEAD` points to?

1.message: Did you inspect the repository metadata and confirm what `HEAD` points to?

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Set a Repository-Only Identity
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

Set the author name to `ZoeSkoul Learner` and the email to `learner@zoeskoul.local` inside `trail-journal/` only. Print both local values afterward.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Keep the author identity inside this repository rather than changing machine-wide settings.

help.concept: Repository-local identity applies only to this project and does not alter the machine-wide Git configuration.
help.hint_1: Both the name and email need repository-local values.
help.hint_2: Read the stored values back to catch spelling or scope mistakes.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.solutionCode:
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
git config --local --get user.name
git config --local --get user.email
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

Set the author name to `ZoeSkoul Learner` and the email to `learner@zoeskoul.local` inside `trail-journal/` only. Print both local values afterward.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.title: Ask Git What It Sees
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

The repository and local identity are ready. Use short status to see the untracked `README.md` before staging anything.
try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.hint: Use the compact repository report and read both status columns.

help.concept: git status --short summarizes the relationship between the working tree and the staging area.
help.hint_1: Focus on the path named in the task and decide whether it is untracked, staged, or modified.
help.hint_2: Compare the final status with the exact state the prompt asks you to prove.

try_configure_and_initialize_a_repositor.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `trail-journal/` project folder.

The repository and local identity are ready. Use short status to see the untracked `README.md` before staging anything.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf trail-journal
mkdir -p trail-journal
cd trail-journal
printf %s '# Trail Journal
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C trail-journal init -q -b main
git -C trail-journal config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C trail-journal config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of Version Control
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of version control systems like Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To track changes and collaborate on projects

multi-choice-1.prompt: Which of the following are components of a Git repository? Select all that apply.

help.concept: Turn an ordinary project folder into a Git repository on `main`, inspect the metadata Git created, and keep the author identity local to that repository.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the setup steps in an order that can actually work.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

sketch0.title: Create the Repository in the Right Place
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
A Git repository begins as an ordinary folder. The important first decision is **which folder owns the history**. Enter the project folder, then initialize it with `main` as the first branch:

```bash
git init -b main
```

Git adds private repository metadata to the current folder. It does not upload the project, alter the visible files, or create a commit. If you initialize one directory too high, Git will treat more of your computer as one project than you intended.

Read the command from left to right: `git init` creates the repository, while `-b main` names the first branch. For now, that single command is enough. The next parts of the chapter introduce safe ways to inspect what Git created.

sketch1.title: What Initialization Adds
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Initialization creates a hidden `.git` directory. Your README and other project files stay where they are; `.git` stores configuration, references, and the objects that will form history later.

You may inspect this directory to understand the model. For example, `.git/HEAD` records which branch is currently checked out. Treat the directory as Git-managed data: reading is useful, but manual edits can corrupt the repository.

A repository can exist before it has any commits. At this point Git knows where history will live, but no snapshot has been recorded yet.

sketch2.title: Give Future Commits an Author
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
Every commit records an author name and email. In a learning workspace, keep that identity inside the current repository instead of changing the whole machine:

```bash
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
```

`--local` is the boundary that matters. It stores the values in this repository’s configuration and leaves unrelated repositories alone.

Configuration prepares future commits; it does not create one. Read the values back after setting them so a spelling mistake does not surface later during the first commit.

Read the stored values back before the first commit:

```bash
git config --local --get user.name
git config --local --get user.email
```

The `--get` form is an inspection step. It prints the repository-local value without changing it.

sketch3.title: Ask Git What It Sees
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
The repository now exists, but the project files still have no recorded history. Ask Git for a compact report:

```bash
git status --short
```

A line such as `?? README.md` means the file exists in the working folder but Git has never tracked it. Nothing changes when you run status. It is an inspection command—a safe way to understand the starting state before you choose what belongs in the first snapshot.

This inspect-first habit will remain useful long after the first repository is created.
